macro_segments.title: Introduction and Recapitulation of the Eruv vs. Shittuf Asymmetry

micro_segments.title: Overview of the mishna's asymmetry and the gemara's attempts to resolve it
Rav's statement on the definition of a maboi and the requirements for leniency with lechi and kora

macro_segments.title: Defining the Minimum Requirements for a Maboi: Rav, Shmuel, and Rabbi Yochanan's Positions

micro_segments.title: Rav's requirement of two courtyards with two houses each versus Shmuel's lenient position
Rabbi Yochanan's even more lenient position allowing even a ruin (horva)
The gemara's discussion of what constitutes a valid space: the distinction between a ruin and a vineyard path
Rav Huna bar Chinana's explanation of Rabbi Yochanan's position through the lens of Rabbi Shimon's teaching

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's Principle: Unified Domains for Vessels and the Concept of Reshut Hayachid

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's teaching on roofs, enclosed lots, and courtyards as unified domains for vessels
Explanation of how vessels that originate in one domain may be carried within that domain without an eruv
The fundamental issue of carrying between different types of reshuyot hayachid and the need for unification
Rashi's expansion of Rabbi Shimon's principle to allow carrying between roofs, enclosed lots, and courtyards as a single abstract category

macro_segments.title: The Gezeirah Concern: When Eruv Creation Prevents Cross-Domain Carrying

micro_segments.title: Rav's position: the gemara's interpretation of the gezeirah against allowing cross-courtyard carrying when an eruv is made
Shmuel and Rabbi Yochanan's rejection of the gezeirah: carrying is permitted regardless of eruv status
The gemara's reframing: the true concern is not a gezeirah but rather that without an eruv, the ruin remains a separate domain

macro_segments.title: Shmuel's Actual Position Reconsidered: The Eivu Bar Ichi Case and the Debate Over Dwelling Place

micro_segments.title: Rav Bruna's citation of Shmuel's lenient view and Rabbi Elazar bar Bei Rav's skeptical challenge
The gemara's response: Shmuel is bound by the language of the mishna, which requires two houses and two courtyards
The case of the chazan who ate at home but slept in the synagogue and the question of whether his dwelling counts
Resolution: Shmuel did not reverse his position; the case involved a second dwelling place (the synagogue), allowing two houses

macro_segments.title: The Complex Case of a Maboi with One Side Inhabited by Non-Jews: Rav's Prohibition

micro_segments.title: Presentation of the case: a maboi with houses opening onto it, one side inhabited by non-Jews and the other by Jews
Rav's prohibition: Jews cannot make an eruv through windows to permit carrying into the maboi
Abaye's question: does this prohibition apply even when the obstruction is a courtyard rather than a maboi?
Rav's affirmative response and the reasoning that the non-Jewish dwelling does not count as a valid domain

macro_segments.title: The Underlying Principle: Prohibition Against Creating a Yachid in the Presence of Non-Jews

micro_segments.title: The real concern: attempting to create a single unified household (yachid) through window connections while a non-Jew inhabits the courtyard
Rav Yosef's understanding: now the repeated emphasis on non-Jews in Rav Tavla's transmission becomes clear
Summary: the prohibition against yachid bimkom nochri (a single household in the presence of a non-Jew) explains the entire case
